description: 8 Confusing Soviet Nuclear Diplomacy By RUSSELL BRINES f WASHINGTON CP The usual uncertainties of Soviet diplomaey will require U S and British negotiators to begin their July 15 nuclear talks in Mozcow without knowing whether Russia is ready for any tesi ban ugreement I This uncertzinty diplomatic sources here say was ereased probably intentionally by Soviet Premier Khrushchevs remarks t on the subject during his July 2 speech in East Berlin The speech presumably constitutes the Kremlins latest official position on a nuclesr moratorium but the diplomats are clearly divided over its exact meaning Fhrushchv said the Soviet government is ready to conclude an agreement oanning nuclear tests in the atmosphere in outer space and under watcr without any inspection system On the surface this adicated Moscow had accepted the latest Anglo American propnsals to conclude this type of moratorium as a means of elimirating the dangers of radioactive fallout and de ereasing world tensions In the same speech however Khrushchev said the Soviet Government belicves s nonaggression pact between Communist Warsaw powers and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization t NATO should be concluded at the same time as the proposed nuclear arrangement Linking these two moves together he
